evidence_quotes.quote: Games are becoming increasingly opaque about what is, for example, the real cost of their digital products.
Concealing the costs of all kinds of digital game internal currencies, which still have to be purchased separately,
Especially in the area of mobile phone games, the practices are predatory!
the provider is not obliged to transfer your account with shopping!

paragraphs.text: I would ask you to include in this project the fact that many consumers are currently being exploited and excluded in the field of online games. — Players sometimes pay more for digital games than for the physical media of those games. — Unfinished games are pre-sold and often not tested before publication. Customers are therefore increasingly paying for unfinished and sometimes even unsafe products, which can overburden and damage the hardware of their devices. — Games are becoming increasingly opaque about what is, for example, the real cost of their digital products. Concealing the costs of all kinds of digital game internal currencies, which still have to be purchased separately, means that entire teams of experienced players have to set themselves up in order to pay for their communities. Often then there are also protests and objections from customers to suppliers because prices go beyond all frameworks, but this is difficult to see at first sight. Because it is often NOT possible to place everything in a shopping trolley, which then calculates exactly what the fun will then cost. There are also elements of gambling rather than open and straight-line pricing. Especially in the area of mobile phone games, the practices are predatory! — many live service games force daily login. Customers often pay for extra parcels, but they need to be collected on a daily basis. If the internet is not available for one day or the customer loses digital goods paid for by customers instead of being transferred to the next day of the log-in (possibly with one month deadline, this would be more feasible). Specifically in the spirit of the mental health of people (especially those whose sheaths are still in development), it would be desirable if there were customer protection, which stipulates that at least purchased (but both purchased and free log-in rewards must be collected for at least 1 week before they can lapse). As a result, no game can be blackmailed in this way. As difficult as I do with China, they have succeeded in establishing that children and adolescents in particular must be able to collect all the week’s log-in rewards by clicking on a train at the weekend. This possibility should also be the rule for adults. Gambling addiction is not a minor problem, and that many customers pay for having to be present on a daily basis is simply not acceptable. — Live Service Games are published and players invest a lot of money and then the games are completely removed from the network or servers are completely removed from the network in the shortest possible time, and although customers can still register to play on the new server, the provider is not obliged to transfer your account with shopping! As a result, countless customers are simply blamed. This is as if you had bought a apartment and then comes after a year, locks all the doors and tells you no longer, without reimbursement of your costs. Games and content no longer belong to customers and are therefore exposed to the activities of the providers. There are games that plan with offline versions and successfully implement so-called ‘end of life’ plans. But there is nothing that obliges providers to make these plans. The citizens’ initiative entitled ‘Stop Killing Games’ summarises this problem well and I hope that this issue will also be addressed here. And this is only a small overview of how online games have lost landscape in the last 15 years and will not be better without regulation. Because if customers complain according to the law, the game is destroyed at the expense of customers and often by games developers. I hope more customer protection!
